questions.q: Of which kingdom was Shakuni a prince?
options: Madra
Gandhara
Sindhu
Kekaya
questions.explanation: Shakuni was a prince of Gandhara, the far northwestern kingdom whose name survives in modern Kandahar.

questions.q: Who was Shakuni's sister?
options: Kunti, mother of the Pandavas
Madri, the second wife of Pandu
Dushala, the wife of Jayadratha
Gandhari, the blindfolded queen
questions.explanation: Shakuni was the brother of Gandhari, who married the blind king Dhritarashtra of Hastinapura.

questions.q: Which nephew did Shakuni constantly counsel and scheme for?
options: Duryodhana
Yudhishthira
Abhimanyu
Ghatotkacha
questions.explanation: Shakuni was the whispering uncle behind Duryodhana, feeding his envy and engineering his plots.

questions.q: What skill made Shakuni infamous throughout the epic?
options: Forging unbreakable weapons
Reading the stars and omens
His mastery of the dice
Brewing untraceable poisons
questions.explanation: Shakuni was a peerless dice player whose throws seemed to obey his will.

questions.q: What scheme of Shakuni's stripped the Pandavas of their kingdom?
options: A forged decree of banishment
The rigged game of dice
A midnight raid on Indraprastha
A false treaty with Panchala
questions.explanation: Shakuni's crooked play at the dice game won Yudhishthira's wealth, kingdom, brothers, and finally Draupadi.

questions.q: Which Pandava killed Shakuni in the war?
options: Sahadeva
Nakula
Bhima
Arjuna
questions.explanation: Sahadeva, the youngest Pandava, slew Shakuni exactly as he had vowed after the dice game.

questions.q: On which day of the Kurukshetra war did Shakuni die?
options: The tenth day
The thirteenth day
The seventeenth day
The eighteenth day
questions.explanation: Shakuni fell on the eighteenth and final day of the war, shortly before Duryodhana's last stand.

questions.q: In the dice game against Yudhishthira, who actually cast the dice for the Kaurava side?
options: Duryodhana, the challenger
Dushasana, his loyal brother
Shakuni, playing on Duryodhana's behalf
Karna, the king of Anga
questions.explanation: Duryodhana supplied the stakes while his uncle Shakuni threw the dice and won every round.

questions.q: By what patronymic is Shakuni often called in the epic?
options: Saubala, son of Subala
Gandharva, son of the north
Kaitava, son of deception
Parvateya, son of the mountains
questions.explanation: Shakuni is frequently called Saubala after his father Subala, the king of Gandhara.

questions.q: What was the family relationship between Shakuni and Duryodhana?
options: Teacher and pupil bound by oath
Maternal uncle and nephew
Foster brothers raised together
Cousins through King Subala
questions.explanation: As Gandhari's brother, Shakuni was Duryodhana's maternal uncle, his mama and closest adviser.

questions.q: What was at stake in the second, final round of dice?
options: The throne of Hastinapura against Indraprastha
Draupadi's freedom against half the kingdom
Thirteen years of exile, the last spent in disguise
A pledge of peace lasting thirty-six years
questions.explanation: The single final throw condemned the losers to twelve years of forest exile and a thirteenth year undiscovered, or the whole term again.

questions.q: Why did Yudhishthira feel unable to refuse the invitation to dice?
options: Shakuni had promised him the game would be fair
He trusted that his luck at dice could never fail
Draupadi and his brothers urged him to accept
Honor forbade refusing a challenge, and his elder had summoned him
questions.explanation: Bound by the kshatriya code never to decline a challenge and by Dhritarashtra's summons, Yudhishthira went despite his misgivings.

questions.q: What event planted the idea of the dice game in Shakuni's mind?
options: Yudhishthira's victories in the Rajasuya conquests
Duryodhana's burning envy after his humiliation at Indraprastha
Bhima's taunts at the tournament of the princes
Krishna's growing friendship with the Pandavas
questions.explanation: After Duryodhana was mocked in Maya's hall of illusions, Shakuni proposed the dice game as a bloodless way to ruin the Pandavas.

questions.q: Who was Uluka, sent to the Pandavas with Duryodhana's insulting message on the eve of war?
options: Shakuni's son
Shakuni's younger brother
Shakuni's charioteer
Shakuni's spymaster
questions.explanation: Uluka was Shakuni's son, dispatched as Duryodhana's envoy to taunt the Pandavas before the fighting began.

questions.q: What happened to Uluka on the war's final day?
options: He fled to Gandhara and survived the war
He surrendered to Yudhishthira at dawn
He fell to Bhima while guarding Duryodhana
He was slain by Sahadeva before Shakuni's eyes
questions.explanation: Sahadeva killed Uluka in his father's sight on the eighteenth day, just before bringing down Shakuni himself.

questions.q: What vow was sworn against Shakuni after the dice game?
options: Arjuna swore to kill him before the war's first sunset
Bhima swore to crush the hand that cast the dice
Sahadeva swore to cut him down in battle
Nakula swore to scatter his dice with arrows
questions.explanation: In the assembly Sahadeva vowed to slay Shakuni, the architect of the deceitful game, and fulfilled it on the war's last day.

questions.q: What declaration did Shakuni famously repeat after each decisive throw?
options: I have won
The dice obey me
Fate favors Gandhara
Stake once more, king
questions.explanation: After every round Shakuni announced that he had won, as stake after stake passed to Duryodhana.

questions.q: In the epic's roll of incarnations, Shakuni embodied what?
options: Kali, the spirit of the darkest age
Dvapara, the spirit of the age of the deuce
The asura Viprachitti reborn
The serpent Takshaka in human form
questions.explanation: The Adi Parva identifies Shakuni as Dvapara incarnate, the spirit of the losing dice-throw, while Duryodhana embodied Kali.

questions.q: What forces did Shakuni command at Kurukshetra?
options: The elephant corps of the eastern kings
The river fleet guarding the supply lines
The Gandhara horsemen of the northwest
The mercenary archers of the southern coast
questions.explanation: Shakuni led the swift cavalry of Gandhara, mountain horsemen who harried the Pandava flanks.

questions.q: How did Shakuni famously describe his dice when promising to win Yudhishthira's wealth without a battle?
options: As gifts from the god of fate himself
As older than the throne of Hastinapura
As pieces carved from his own destiny
As his bow and arrows, his skill of battle
questions.explanation: In the Sabha Parva, Shakuni boasted that the dice were his bows and arrows, the heart of the dice his bowstring, and the dice-board his chariot — gambling was his form of war.

questions.q: During Krishna's peace embassy, Shakuni was among those who backed which plan of Duryodhana's?
options: To seize and imprison Krishna the envoy
To poison the envoy's evening meal
To ambush the envoy on the road home
To bribe the envoy with Gandhara gold
questions.explanation: Duryodhana plotted with his inner circle to capture Krishna, a scheme foiled when Krishna revealed his cosmic form.

questions.q: What was the last stake Yudhishthira wagered and lost at Shakuni's urging in the first game?
options: His own freedom, after losing his brothers
Draupadi, after he had staked even himself
The crown jewels of Indraprastha
The royal treasury and his chariots
questions.explanation: Having lost his wealth, his brothers, and his own self, Yudhishthira finally staked Draupadi, and Shakuni won again.

questions.q: What weapon ended Shakuni's life?
options: Sahadeva's sword in a duel fought on foot
A spear hurled from Nakula's chariot
A gold-winged broad-headed arrow from Sahadeva's bow
A mace blow delivered beside the lake
questions.explanation: Sahadeva severed Shakuni's head with a broad-headed arrow winged with gold in the final day's fighting.

questions.q: With what did Sahadeva taunt Shakuni at the moment of killing him?
options: The memory of Uluka's flight from battle
The exile's years counted out like throws
The blindfold his sister wore for life
The dice of the gambling hall, now answered by arrows
questions.explanation: Sahadeva reminded Shakuni of the dice he had cast in the assembly hall, declaring that arrows would now repay those throws.

questions.q: When Sahadeva struck down his son Uluka, how did Shakuni respond?
options: He wept and charged at Sahadeva in grief and fury
He fled toward Duryodhana's last stronghold
He offered Sahadeva his dice as ransom
He called on Ashwatthama to avenge the boy
questions.explanation: Maddened by grief at Uluka's death, Shakuni rushed upon Sahadeva and was slain in that final clash.

questions.q: Early in the war, several of Shakuni's brothers were slain when they attacked which warrior?
options: Abhimanyu, the son of Subhadra
Iravan, Arjuna's son by the naga princess Ulupi
Ghatotkacha, the giant son of Bhima
Shikhandi, the reborn princess of Kashi
questions.explanation: On the eighth day the Gandhara princes, sons of Subala, fell upon Iravan and were cut down by him before he himself was slain.

questions.q: In popular later retellings, what were Shakuni's unbeatable dice said to be made from?
options: Ivory taken from the king of elephants
The knucklebones of a defeated rival king
The wood of a tree struck by lightning
The bones of his dead father, Subala
questions.explanation: The tale that the dice were carved from Subala's bones and obeyed his son is a popular folk addition, absent from Vyasa's Sanskrit epic, where Shakuni simply plays with deceitful skill.

questions.q: By the time Shakuni fell on the eighteenth day, what was true of the Kaurava leadership?
options: Bhishma and Drona still directed the armies from the field
Karna had withdrawn wounded to the royal camp
Shalya had already fallen, leaving Duryodhana all but alone
Ashwatthama had been crowned the supreme commander
questions.explanation: Yudhishthira had killed the commander Shalya that morning, so Shakuni's death left Duryodhana with almost no leaders standing.

questions.q: Who was Shakuni's father?
options: Subala, the king of Gandhara
Suvira, the lord of the hills
Sudakshina of Kamboja
Susharma of Trigarta
questions.explanation: Shakuni was the son of King Subala of Gandhara, which is why the epic calls him Saubala.

questions.q: Why did Yudhishthira protest before agreeing that Shakuni would throw the dice?
options: He suspected the dice were enchanted by demons
He wanted Vidura to inspect the dice beforehand
He had vowed to gamble only against crowned kings
It seemed crooked for a substitute to play on Duryodhana's behalf
questions.explanation: Yudhishthira objected that one man staking while another played was irregular, but Shakuni brushed the objection aside and the game went on.
